Your transaction data shows two active 401(k) contributions running simultaneously, which is a great foundation to evaluate. Here's where things stand based on your May payroll records.
Current contribution picture
You're contributing to two separate 401(k) plans right now:
Salesforce 401(k): $700 per semi-monthly paycheck — that's 8% of your $8,750 gross base pay. Annualized, this projects to $16,800 per year ($700 × 24 pay periods).
UCHealth 401(k): $300 per semi-monthly paycheck — that's 6% of your $5,000 gross base pay. Annualized, this projects to $7,200 per year ($300 × 24 pay periods).
Combined annual 401(k) contributions: $16,800 + $7,200 = $24,000 per year
How this compares to the 2026 limit
The 2026 IRS elective deferral limit across all 401(k) plans combined is $24,500. Since your two plans are both under your name, the IRS applies a single aggregate limit — meaning your $24,000 combined total leaves you just $500 under the annual cap.
That's actually a very efficient position — you're at approximately 98% of the allowable limit. The practical gap to close is minimal.
One important flag to address
Your two paychecks deposit into the same checking account, and you're contributing across two employer plans simultaneously. A few things worth confirming:
Are you a dual W-2 employee simultaneously at both Salesforce and UCHealth? If so, the $24,500 aggregate limit still applies across both plans combined. You're currently on pace to contribute $24,000 total, which keeps you safely under the limit.
Employer match status: Your transaction data doesn't show employer match deposits directly, but this is significant — if each employer offers even a 3–4% match, that could represent an additional $3,000–$5,000 in annual contributions above your own deferrals (matches don't count against your $24,500 limit).
If cashflow allows — what to change
Since this involves tax planning decisions, I'll explain these educationally. Per CFP Board standards, consult a qualified tax advisor for advice specific to your situation before making decisions.
Given your combined gross income appears to be approximately $163,500 annually ($105,000 from Salesforce + $60,000 from UCHealth, based on the payroll data), here's where to focus:
Close the $500 gap: The simplest improvement is adding $500 more across either plan to fully max out your elective deferrals. This could be done by bumping the UCHealth deferral from 6% to approximately 7% (adding ~$300/year) and slightly increasing the Salesforce rate.
HSA contribution opportunity: Your HealthEquity HSA shows a $400 contribution in May. If you're on a family high-deductible health plan (HDHP), the 2026 family HSA limit is $8,750. At $400/month you'd reach $4,800 annually — still $3,950 below the family limit. The HSA is a triple-tax-advantaged account (pre-tax contributions, tax-free growth, tax-free qualified withdrawals), making it arguably the most efficient savings vehicle available to you.
Colorado 529: Your $300/month contribution to Colorado 529 CollegeInvest is solid. Colorado residents receive a state income tax deduction on 529 contributions, so this is working efficiently alongside your retirement savings.
Roth vs. pre-tax consideration: At your income level, your marginal federal bracket is likely 22–24%. Since both current 401(k) plans appear to be traditional (pre-tax), it may be worth asking each employer if they offer a Roth 401(k) option — particularly useful if you expect to be in a higher bracket in retirement.
--ur overall retirement savings discipline is strong 💪. The most impactful next step is confirming your employer match rates — are you capturing the full match at both Salesforce and UCHealth?
